title: From Movie Prop to Monument: The Rocky Statue Gets Its Own Art Exhibition
summary:
You've probably seen the scene: Rocky Balboa runs up the steps of the Philadelphia Museum of Art, arms raised in victory. At the top stands a bronze statue of the fictional boxer. Millions of people from around the world visit it every year, taking photos and touching his gloves for good luck. But how did a movie prop (an object used in a film) become one of America's most beloved monuments?

A new exhibition at the Philadelphia Museum of Art, titled "Rising Up: Rocky and the Making of Monuments," tries to answer that question. It features over 150 works by more than 50 artists, spanning more than two millennia (2,000 years). You'll see ancient Greek sculptures of boxers, nineteenth-century European paintings, and images from the golden age of boxing in the U.S. Contemporary artists like Andy Warhol, Jean-Michel Basquiat, Glenn Ligon, Hank Willis Thomas, and Lisa Brice revisit boxing through the lens of race, gender, and celebrity.

The exhibition explores why people project their hopes onto the Rocky statue. "Perseverance, spirit, and grit" are the values it represents—values shaped by the history of the sport and by lived struggle and aspiration.

The show is organized by Paul Farber, director and co-founder of Monument Lab and host of the NPR/WHYY podcast "The Statue." A companion book includes contributions from Philadelphia artist Alex Da Corte, former Philadelphia Eagle and Super Bowl champion Malcolm Jenkins, and film critic Carrie Rickey. The exhibition runs through May 30. If you're in Philadelphia, don't miss it!
why_it_matters: This exhibition challenges us to think about what makes something a monument and how popular culture can shape public symbols. It encourages critical reflection on the values we admire and the stories we tell through statues.

background_read: The Rocky statue was originally created for the film 'Rocky III' (1982) by sculptor A. Thomas Schomberg.
After filming, it was donated to the Philadelphia Museum of Art and became a popular tourist attraction.
Monument Lab, co-founded by Paul Farber, is a research organization that studies public monuments and their meanings.
This exhibition is part of a broader trend of museums exploring the intersection of popular culture and art.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Description of Rocky running up steps and the statue.
└─ specific: 'How did a movie prop become a beloved monument?'
EXHIBITION: 'Rising Up: Rocky and the Making of Monuments' at Philadelphia Museum of Art.
├─ SCALE: Over 150 works by more than 50 artists spanning 2,000 years.
└─ CONTENTS: Ancient Greek boxer sculptures, 19th-century paintings, photos from boxing golden age, contemporary artists (Warhol, Basquiat, etc.).
THEMATIC FOCUS: Explores why people project hopes onto the statue.
└─ VALUES: Perseverance, spirit, and grit shaped by history of sport and lived struggle.
ORGANIZER: Paul Farber, director of Monument Lab and host of 'The Statue' podcast.
COMPANION BOOK: Contributions from artist Alex Da Corte, NFL champion Malcolm Jenkins, film critic Carrie Rickey.

perspectives.description: The exhibition celebrates a unifying symbol that brings diverse people together and highlights the positive message of hard work and resilience.
Some critics might argue that the Rocky statue is a commercial prop, not a genuine historical monument, and that the exhibition avoids deeper critiques of boxing's violence or racial politics.
The exhibition demonstrates how museums are increasingly engaging with pop culture to attract audiences and provoke discussion about what we choose to memorialize.
As public conversations about monuments and statues continue, exhibitions like this could pave the way for more inclusive and diverse representations of heroism and achievement.
